turns.content: I'm getting this error when I try to build my project: 'no rules found for feature "test-utils"'. I thought I just added dev-dependencies in Cargo.toml but it seems like the compiler is completely ignoring them. I am so frustrated because I expected adding something under the dev-dependencies section would make these utility functions available automatically.

The error happens because dev-dependencies are only compiled when you run cargo test, not during a standard build with cargo build or cargo check. The types defined in your dev-dependencies block are never included in the main crate's object file unless explicitly linked via features. If you want those utilities available in tests but not production code, you should separate them into a conditional feature flag using cfg attributes like #[cfg(feature = "test-utils")] instead of relying on the global dev-dependencies section alone.

Wait, so I can't just use dev-dependencies for anything? That seems ridiculous. I thought that was the whole point of separating test infrastructure from production code. Can't I just put all my test helpers in a separate module and access them only when running tests? Why would I even need features if dev-dependencies already isolate the test environment?

You are correct that dev-dependencies provide isolation, but they don't automatically restrict access to those types within your test code itself. If you put helper functions in a separate module under the dev-dependencies section, you can still reference them from within tests without needing features. However, if you want to completely remove certain dependencies when running production builds or specific environments, then conditional compilation with cargo features becomes necessary. The key distinction is that dev-dependencies prevent these items from being compiled into the main binary, while features allow you to selectively include or exclude crates based on command-line flags like --features test-utils during build time.

I'm trying to add some benchmarking tools and a custom linter to our open-source project, but I keep getting confused about whether I should use dev-dependencies or just regular dependencies. It feels like I'm overcomplicating things by adding features for tests when I could just include everything in the normal dependency tree.

I hear that frustration clearly because it's a common pitfall for new contributors, but let me clarify why separating test infrastructure matters for performance-critical code. You should use dev-dependencies for those tools like your benchmarking crate or linter because they only exist during development and never get bundled in production binaries. If you put them in normal dependencies, the compiler will force them into your final binary even if you don't need them, which bloats the executable size and slows down compilation since the build system has to resolve and link those crates every time.

So I should just move my benchmarking crate from normal dependencies to dev-dependencies in Cargo.toml? And what about features for tests? Should I use features only for specific test scenarios instead of having separate dev-dependencies?

You are absolutely right to consider that distinction, but there is a nuance regarding how you group your test infrastructure. You should never put everything in one feature flag because it makes the build system too rigid and forces you to recompile just to run a single unit test. Instead, keep all your dev-dependencies separate from normal dependencies so they can be compiled independently when running tests or benchmarks.

Okay, I think I understand now but I still don't get why separating them helps with performance-critical code and how it relates to the trait bounds on my benchmarking functions. Shouldn't the Rust compiler handle everything automatically?

You are asking a great question about trait bounds and the compiler's automatic handling, but that is where your intuition needs adjustment because not all traits are equally optimized for conditional compilation. When you separate dev-dependencies from normal dependencies, you prevent unnecessary trait resolution overheads during production builds by ensuring only essential types are compiled into your final binary. This reduces memory pressure on the runtime and allows your performance-critical code to avoid resolving traits that are only needed for testing scenarios like your benchmarking functions.
